text: The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis, exposing the anterior aspect of the promontory on the right side of the sigmoid mesentery using scissors with monopolar cautery. The old mesh is visible, fixed to the right part of the promontory and the lateral part of the middle rectum. The peritoneum over the Douglas pouch is dissected down to the levator ani, with the height of the initial mesh compared to the operator's finger during a digital rectal exam.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis, exposing the anterior aspect of the promontory on the right side of the sigmoid mesentery using scissors with monopolar cautery. The old mesh is visible, fixed to the right part of the promontory and the lateral part of the middle rectum. The peritoneum over the Douglas pouch is dissected until reaching the levator ani, with the mesh's height demonstrated relative to the operator's finger during a digital rectal exam.
The surgeon introduces a bioprosthetic collagen porcine mesh and secures it to the anterior aspect of the lower rectum using five non-absorbable polyethylene-coated braided polyester stitches.

A bioprosthetic collagen porcine mesh is introduced and fixed to the anterior aspect of the lower rectum using five non-absorbable polyethylene-coated braided polyester stitches. The mesh is then fastened to the old mesh with two non-absorbable sutures. The surgeon dissects the promontory, leaving the old mesh in place, and exposes the anterior sacroiliac ligament at the sacral promontory, securing the mesh to the sacrum with a non-absorbable stitch.

The surgeon fastens the new mesh to the old mesh using two non-absorbable sutures. The promontory is dissected, leaving the old mesh in place, and the anterior sacroiliac ligament at the sacral promontory is exposed. A non-absorbable stitch secures the mesh to the sacrum. The Douglas pouch is closed, allowing passage for the sigmoid colon, and the peritoneum is closed over the meshes using two continuous barbed absorbable sutures to isolate them from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon closes the Douglas pouch, creating a passage for the sigmoid colon. The peritoneum is then closed over the meshes using two continuous barbed absorbable sutures, effectively isolating them from the abdominal cavity.
